quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Polyprotic acid"?

options.text: An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6
Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area

quiz_questions.explanation: "Polyprotic acid" is defined as: An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Successive Ka values"?

options.text: Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C; derived from the autoionization of water: 2H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻
Properties that depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity: ΔTb, ΔTf, π, ΔP
The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed
Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion

quiz_questions.explanation: "Successive Ka values" is defined as: Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Diprotic acid"?

options.text: The slowest elementary step in a mechanism; acts as a 'bottleneck' that controls the overall reaction rate
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
An acid with two ionizable protons (e.g., H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃, oxalic acid H₂C₂O₄)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Diprotic acid" is defined as: An acid with two ionizable protons (e.g., H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃, oxalic acid H₂C₂O₄). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Zwitterion"?

options.text: A balanced equation that includes the enthalpy change (ΔH); coefficients represent moles, and the physical state of each species must be specified
A molecule with both positive and negative charges; amino acids exist as zwitterions at their isoelectric point (pH = ½(pKa1 + pKa2))
A species produced in one elementary step and consumed in a subsequent step; does not appear in the overall equation
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve

quiz_questions.explanation: "Zwitterion" is defined as: A molecule with both positive and negative charges; amino acids exist as zwitterions at their isoelectric point (pH = ½(pKa1 + pKa2)). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Isoelectric point (pI)"?

options.text: An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
The pH at which a molecule has zero net charge; for amino acids, pI = ½(pKa1 + pKa2)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Isoelectric point (pI)" is defined as: The pH at which a molecule has zero net charge; for amino acids, pI = ½(pKa1 + pKa2). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)"?

options.text: Lewis acid/base
Pressure/volume change
Solubility product (Ksp)
Polyprotic acid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Polyprotic acid".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion"?

options.text: Heterogeneous catalyst
ΔG° = -nFE°
First law of thermodynamics
Successive Ka values

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Successive Ka values".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An acid with two ionizable protons (e.g., H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃, oxalic acid H₂C₂O₄)"?

options.text: ΔS prediction rules
Selective precipitation
Catalyst
Diprotic acid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Diprotic acid".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A molecule with both positive and negative charges; amino acids exist as zwitterions at their isoelectric point (pH = ½(pKa1 + pKa2))"?

options.text: Catalyst effect
Secondary battery
Zwitterion
Excess reagent

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Zwitterion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The pH at which a molecule has zero net charge; for amino acids, pI = ½(pKa1 + pKa2)"?

options.text: Ka (acid dissociation constant)
Fractional distillation
Isoelectric point (pI)
Factors affecting rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Isoelectric point (pI)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Polyprotic Acids is accurate?

options.text: Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.
Polyprotic Acids can donate more than one proton per molecule, with successive Ka values decreasing (Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3).
Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
Alloys (mixtures of metals) modify properties by introducing different-sized atoms.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Polyprotic Acids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) has a strong first dissociation and weak second (Ka2 = 0.012).
Alloys (mixtures of metals) modify properties by introducing different-sized atoms.
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.
Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Polyprotic Acids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) has three dissociations.
Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
For redox reactions, the half-reaction method balances electron transfer in acidic or basic solutions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Polyprotic Acids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.
Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.
For most polyprotic acid calculations, the first dissociation dominates the pH.

This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Polyprotic Acids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.
Phase changes (melting, boiling, sublimation, condensation, freezing, deposition) occur at specific temperatures where energy input/removal overcomes or establishes IMFs.

Types of Chemical Reactions are classified into several categories: synthesis (A + B → AB), decomposition (AB → A + B), single replacement (A + BC → AC + B), double replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB), and combustion (fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O).
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Polyprotic Acids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Freeze-drying (lyophilization) of food and pharmaceuticals using sublimation under reduced pressure to remove water while preserving structure and nutrients
Displacement reactions in metallurgy: extracting copper by placing iron in CuSO₄ solution (Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, since Fe is more active than Cu)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Forensic analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify and quantify drugs, poisons, and accelerants in criminal investigations
Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Amino acid behavior in gel electrophoresis: proteins migrate based on charge at a given pH relative to their isoelectric points
Displacement reactions in metallurgy: extracting copper by placing iron in CuSO₄ solution (Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, since Fe is more active than Cu)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Polyprotic Acids applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Phosphoric acid in food industry (E338): used in cola drinks (pH ~2.5) and as a rust remover due to its triprotic acid chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Polyprotic Acids in practice?

options.text: Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics
Lewis acid catalysis in organic synthesis: BF₃ and AlCl₃ as Lewis acids in Friedel-Crafts reactions for pharmaceutical manufacturing
Amino acid behavior in gel electrophoresis: proteins migrate based on charge at a given pH relative to their isoelectric points
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.
